transcript.transcript: Do you mind stating your name for the record? Yeah, no problem. Just call me like Mr. Kang. Thank you. Can you say your full name? Chang Hun. Last name, Khang. When did you come to Los Angeles? I came here, 1997. 1997. My age is 25 years old. From Korea. I was working for Samsung in 1985. Okay. So I worked there about one and a half years. But my girlfriend wants to immigrate. My girlfriend's family, whole family, immigrate. So she was my first love. I couldn't lose her. So, I quit, and I followed her to America. Aw. Is that right? Yes. Wow. Then, you know the LA riot? Yes. We have a LA riot, 1992, right? Yes. My father-in law—so my wife, I married in 1986, with her, okay? Okay. So you were still in Korea when you married her? Yes. She come back to marry me. Oh, she came back to marry you! Just to marry. So she was here already? Yes. When I come here, she—one year before. And then my wife's father was a victim of LA riots. Oh, no. So she got the fire on her business. He used to have a restaurant, too. That restaurant burned down. The government make a disaster loan, FEMA. So I'm the eldest son-in-law. The other siblings or brothers, all of them, is a student, so I have to help him. So we got the SBA loan from the government because of the LA riots, and we opened this restaurant 1992, my father-in-law. By my father in law. Okay. And then that was the first all first all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue. 1992. 1992? Yes. So first time, we're doing pretty good. And then other restaurants take a look at us. And they copy our, you know, the business style, but they cannot do like a buffet. So they just change, that is all you can eat. So the difference between ours and theirs is they serve. But this one is the customer pick up by themselves, cook by themselves, and whatever they want, they can pick how much they want. They can pick. You know what I mean? Yeah. But all you can eat is, you know, just the customer can eat what they serve. We have many kind of barbecue, and then they make some fusion food or something like that. But we are really, really authentic Korean food, like pumpkin porridge and wrapped with a lettuce wrap. And acorn jelly. Yes. Love acorn jelly, actually. Yeah. Dotori-muk. Like real Korean food. So I know somebody like fusion food. But we truly want to let them know about Korean food. Real Korean food. So it was pretty okay. But in 1998, something like that, my father-in-law and my mother-in-law, they want to retire. So my wife have to handle this. Oh, yeah? When my father opened this business, my wife was working here like a owner, instead of her father. But actually, legal owner changed in 1998 to my wife. I see, so you formalized. Yes. So, like, same form, same style. But we're keeping—make food, more variety. More menus and then trying to do very clean. And kind to customers. So it was pretty good. But we get over the, you know, 1998 IMF in Korea. We have a lot of travelers here. So 1998, there was IMF in Korea. No traveler coming at all. So that time we had a hard time. Oh. And 2004, you know, crazy cow disease. Do you know that? Yeah, yeah, yeah. That happened. Nobody eat the meat. Mad cow, yes! Mad cow, yes. 2004. That's right. Yes. We, me and my wife— I am a realtor, actually. Selling houses. So she had some difficulties. She already discussed the difficulties with me. So, we thinking together, what should I do? But we get over the mad cow disease. 2008, Lehman Brothers. Right? 2008. Yeah. Horrible, for everybody. Financial crisis. So that time, we get over it. Right now, I think from the start right now, it's about 32 years. As you see, I don't know, as you see, interior is pretty old, but we make it clean. [laughs] You know what I mean? I do know what you mean. It is charming about it, though. It's not fantastic. It's not something automatic or something, like, you know, new order system. No, we don't have that one. Okay? No TV. We just have music like 1980s, 1990s. Right? So, yeah. Still. Yeah. Still working like that. Yeah. But right now, I really, really had a hard time. No kidding. I could believe it. I don't know what's the reason, but you know what? Right now, all restaurant business. All restaurants, yeah. Collapse. And then all other—employees costs going up, rent. Rent goes up. All the material goes up. Hardly, I cannot resist right now. Almost. Yeah. I mean, almost at the end in my position right now. I feel like I'm the edge of the cliff. Somebody just push, then I can fall down. Very stressful. I'm so sorry. Yeah. So anyway, still I'm doing my best to do this. Yeah. It is a lot. Yeah. But I love these business. Yeah. I'm so happy. The customer satisfied with our service, and they say, 'Oh, delicious. I'm so happy with this meal.' Customer say it like this, I'm also happy too. And then, you know, my wife? I met her when I was 19 year old freshman in Korea. She was so beautiful. I don't know, somebody didn't say like that, but for me, she was so beautiful. She was all to me. That's why I quit everything in Korea and come to America to start working like a dishwasher or something like that. Just start all over. As I say, I'm working, the Samsung. So my father, my family is really, really against our marriage. Really? Yes. But, you know, I say I cannot live without her just one day. So I'm sorry, mom, I have to marry. I come to America. I don't know. It was really nice for—you know, the best thing I do in my life—I'm still thinking same like that. The best thing I did in my life for me is the marriage with her. I'm still thinking like that. Okay? I'm going to cry. That's so sweet. But I lost everything in 2019. So at the funeral, I promised to everybody, I'm going to keep and protect whatever she left to me. So after she left me, I decide, I take over this restaurant. I see. I was just helping, you know, whole years. But as the owner, I'm doing this as a pretty, pretty hard job to me. So hard. Nobody help me. I don't know any food. I know I used to work the white collar in Korea. I never been in kitchen. Yeah. I was going to ask, what did you do at Samsung? Samsung? When you were at Samsung, what was your job there? It's a sales department. Sales department. Okay. Very different than a restaurant. Yeah, yeah. Never been in kitchen, you know. In the Korean custom the man—it doesn't look good, man working in kitchen. That's a Korean custom. So, 2020 and Corona coming. Pandemic stopped. So many things. So the chef quit job, so I really really had a headache. Shut down the business. This is a buffet. So we cannot make like, you know, outside street food. I cannot make it, but I change, like a lunchbox. Okay. Okay. Still a good idea. That's a good idea. Selling 100 [in] one day. Cost about 300, one day. Okay. Losing money. Yes. But I keep on open this business during the pandemic, too. And then there was no chef. So I starting the food. Do you know kimchi? I do know kimchi. Yes. All different types of kimchi. Nobody teach me how to make it. So what did you do? Did you learn? I studied. I studied and go and try, tens of times. Yeah. Then I make it. Then you made it. Everybody say it's delicious. Oh, good! 'Really? It works?' So after that, I make other Korean food by myself. I think to keep this business, I did all I can do. More than I can do. But after my wife left, this business, during the pandemic, I got about, 400 to 500,000 from the government. Okay. And that was the loan. And then the support is around 300,000. Okay. So total, more than $800,000 after pandemic start. Nothing I have right now. That means that was all the loss. But still, my employee is working happy, they can make money. Even though I don't get any profit from this business. And then, you know, I'm here. I can still feel my wife in here. So as I told you, I'm doing a realty business. So for my life, I can make money from the real estate business, so I can manage my life selling home, buying home with commission. But from this business, for about 4 to 5 years, I couldn't make any dollars from this business. But, you know, I come here 365 days a year. And you're working probably 18 hours a day. Yes. And then, after my wife left me, I went to my wife every morning for 5 years. So in the morning, go to my wife and come here. That was my normal—that used to be my routine. But after 5 years, I'm angry, you know? It's getting harder, all the situations. Yeah. I'm angry at—I think after five years, I think I really want something happen to me. What do you mean? Like something like ...? I want her back. Crazy, huh? No. Not crazy. I'm crazy. Not crazy. I want her back. Something, you know, in the movie. Yeah. You travel and then you bring them back. I want something happen. But it doesn't change anything in real life. So I think I have to—no, no, I don't want to forget about her. I just keep remembering her. But my life should be changed. Not like this way, for the future. Okay. I understand. So, anyway. All the businesses have to make a profit. Yeah. And especially restaurant, to make a profit, material cost should be 30% or less. That's what they say, yes. I've heard that too. Okay. Our restaurant material cost very high. Is that because the meat is high? That means we are using a very good materials. We don't, you know, make false—we say, this is short rib, then short ribs. Yeah. But some restaurants say this is short ribs, but they don't make this short rib with short rib. Something else make it looks like a short rib. That means material costs are very high. That means, very good price, I mean, for the customer. And then—I don't know what to say. That's okay. I'll ask you more questions if it helps. So, I don't know. Keeping trying to save the money. Anything, you know, if I can I would do it by myself, to fix something. You know, air conditioning problems. I'm not a technician, but, you know. YouTube, find out how to fix it. Yeah, I do most of them. I did it to save cost. Yeah. I'm telling you right now, almost, it is in my limit, so I don't know how long I can survive more. Yes, I totally understand that. It's so hard, and you've been doing this for so long. And it's one reason why I like talking to you for this. That means in the future, somebody can listen back to it, too, you know, and learn from it as well. Just a quick question: So you and your wife took this in— or your father-in-law at '92, is that right? But the restaurant opened in '85. Was there another owner? Yes. Another owner. That was not the Korean barbecue. Oh, it wasn't a Korean barbecue. That's a Korean food buffet. Ah, okay. Not this grill in the table. Okay, so what did they—was it seafood? Was it vegetables? Korean food. Just Korean food? Yes. What kind of Korean food? Short ribs. Here is the raw Korean short ribs: Right now, pick up short ribs here. But at that time, there was a corner, there was one grill, somebody standing there, barbecue, and [if] customer want it, pick up. Oh, I see. And then this restaurant changed like 1991, something like that, with this style. Oh. And right after, I don't know what happened, the former owner leaving here, so we take over this right away. Oh, I see, I see. So it was just a buffet. You go there, they cook it for you, you pick it up. Yes. That's the first Korean buffet, too. Oh, is that right? That was the first Korean buffet? Wow. 1985. The same name. Just change to the all-you-can-eat barbecue, barbecue buffet. Right? That makes sense. So they were the first buffet—because I also have seen other buffets. So they started the buffet idea, other people started doing that, too. Interesting. Do you know how they got that idea to do that? Was that like a popular way of eating, with the buffet? I don't know. At that time, that time is in Korea, all-you-can-eat going to start. Ah, I see. So then they brought it here. I see. And they show us how to do, then, you know. I see. Do you remember what else they served, besides short rib? Excuse me? Do you remember anything else they served, besides the short rib? Was it soup? When? The first time? The first time. Like a porridge. Porridge, yeah. Pumpkin porridge. And I cannot remember. I just remember the short rib over there and you know bindaetteok? Yeah. Do you know bindaetteok? I do know bindaetteok. Yeah, bindaetteok served over there. That's two things I can remember that time I was here as a customer. As a customer? Yes. Okay. All right. Did you like it? Yeah. The point is, my mother-in-law, whenever she came here to eat, she said, 'Ah, I want to do business like this. I want to do business like this.' She always said, when she came here as a customer. Finally, they want to do this. So first they start. I said it's too much risk. I'm against it. Yeah. Because you're not wrong. It's very—a lot of risk. I made a, you know, report. Four to 6 months: How much should be, how much expected cost. And then if we cannot sell this within 6 months, we have to close. I explained to my father-in-law, mother-in-law. But she still want it. So, okay. Let's go. Wow. Did they do restaurants before? Was this the first restaurant? Yeah. They did it before. They did do it before. That's why she— burned down the restaurant and the LA riot. Oh yeah, okay. Okay. It was her restaurant. Yes, that's right, I remember. But none like this. Yeah. Wow. Something very small. Okay. When you took it over, when you and your wife took it over, did you do all you can eat with the meat? Did you do that right away, or did you keep the same menu as it was before? What do you mean? Like the menu, was it the same—when you when you started the restaurant, when you took it over, did you keep the menu the same or did you change the menu? Yeah. Same menu. Same menu. And then the grills were already here. Yes. So you put the meat out, and people would stock up and eat. Yes. What was the response? In the beginning, you said six months. Did it make a profit in six months? Did it meet the your plan? So. 1992, around November. First month, we make a profit. Wow. First month? Yes. Wow. Wow. Who was your customers? Korean. Yeah. First time? Most of Korean, but right now, 50/50. Okay. Can I ask you about in the very—in the beginning, when you started the all you can eat, what did you serve? What was on the table for people to get? I know you had soup. You had different types of meat? Yes. What kind of meat did you— Bulgogi. Almost the same— As now? Yeah, but we add more later. Oh, so what did you add? What did you add later? At that time, the main is the short ribs. Right now, we add boneless short ribs. And then many things, the Korean sides. Oh, like the banchan? Yeah, banchan. We add more. And then something like a pineapple, fruit. That was a can, but right now it's fresh. Nice. Yeah. We add some side and more items. Right now, we have—there is tongue, and there is shrimp and baby octopus. That's all added and new. And acorn jelly and squid, fried. Something we add, keep on more variety. Right. What's your most popular thing? What does everybody always go and get? Kimchi. Your kimchi that you made. Yeah. Yeah. Somebody want me to sell it, but no, no, I just give them. Is there a secret to your kimchi? You don't have to say if it's a secret. But is there something special that you do to make it so good? I saw the YouTube. Around 50. I look at all the video, and I think which one I can do. Okay. There are many ways to make kimchi, but which one? The YouTuber, he's a very specialist about the food so I cannot—something I cannot do, whatever she did. So I try to figure out which one I can do and take out and mix it all the ways. Yeah. Mix and make it my own recipe. And try just one piece. Try. Everybody say good. Okay. Make it three. Good. Okay. One box. Good. All right. Three box Yeah. That's most of the food, I just learn from the YouTube. Yeah, but you must have sohn-mat. Everything I saw the YouTube, I make my own recipe. You know, the sikhye? Sikhye? Sikhye. Sweet rice drink. Oh, yeah. That's my own recipe, too. Is that right? Yeah. Oh. Everybody say, 'How could you make that?' Yeah, it's pretty hard. I said, but, you know, the sweet rice drink is very hard to make it. Yeah. The Korean traditional drink. But everybody say that's better than before. Before means the chef making. Mine is better than chef make it. Kimchi and cold noodle, too. I make it. You make it. So. Anyway, I try my best. It sounds like it's pretty good, your best. It's pretty good. Before, was it all your wife's recipes? Your family-in-law's recipes? My mother-in-law take care of the taste. Okay. Yeah. My wife is just administration operation business and take care of the customers. You know, some customers came here, they make me crazy. She always laugh with the customers. So some 14 people come here about five years, six years? 'Hi. Long time no see. Where is your wife?' So I say. They couldn't eat, with the crying. So they make me crazy, too. Yeah. Of course. Yeah. Anyway, that was good attitude to the customer, my wife. Anyway. Yeah. That's the point. I cannot do better than my wife. The talking to people? [laughs] Yeah. I cannot do—I did whatever with numbering or whatever, but—no, not good. I think I'm very friendly, but ... It's a different thing. It's different to walk around like that. Do you think when people come here today, do they know how to do this? Have you noticed that more people know how to do this? Or do you still think that you need to teach people how to work the Korean barbecue and make their own? So my wife never did it. But my major is business administration. I want contact with the customers. So when they're sitting here, they are eating I'm going around. And then when I take a look at the table, I think I know what they need. What is missing? Different than server. So I go to the table: 'I think you might need something, like salt and pepper, oil.' Then, 'Yeah, you want to try? Okay. Green onion. This green onion, we can make, like, a sweet sour spice. You want?' 'Yeah. Please.' Then we make it. And then sometime, they pick up the lettuce, but they don't bring the bean paste or something like that. Then I go to the table: 'Can I show you the Korean way to eat the barbecue?' 'Yeah, sure.' Then I bring the bean paste, garlic, the green onion here. Then you know, with a glove. 'Look at us here. Meat. This is marinated so you don't have to dip the salt and pepper. If it's pork belly, beef belly, something like that, you should put the dipping, the salt and pepper. Okay. Here. And then green onion here. The jalapeño, dipping the bean paste. Garlic, dipping the bean paste. All together. It's ready to go.' But my style. My own style, I want little rice added here. Oh! Steamed rice. So steamed rice like this much. Then, say, 'There's one last step. Finish by this one bite. So to do that, this one, make it small. But remember, that is not what I'm saying.' You know the Hugh Jackman, X-Men? Yes. He invited his friend home. He prepared the Korean food. He explained how to eat Korean barbecue with the lettuce. He did lots of work, was finished by one bite. He said, okay? That's a real YouTube. Hugh Jackman, the YouTube. That's so funny. 'Oh, really?' Then they try one bite. Then I say, 'Like this. Every bite is going to be different taste in your mouth.' 'Oh yeah. Yeah, yeah.' Then they bring the lettuce more, some other things, many things. There is, you know, onion, cilantro, and salt. Something I like the way, very delicious, then I want to tell them. So there's onion, cilantro, and sauce. Bring here and small bowl. Bring this one here. Dipping, and this onion here together. Then they try it all together. 'Oh. It's good!' I saw it like that. Wow. Yeah. Wow. That's amazing. That sounds so good. You know, when people started grilling it themselves, were there a lot of questions about that? Or is that something that people just already, because most of your customers are Korean, so they already knew how to do it? Yeah, most of them, they knew it. But the server—server, first time, they put the meat in here. So, they serve it. We bring the scissors and the tong. Most of them, they do it. Oh I see, I see. So they had no problem doing that. They would just get more meat whatever, and they come back. Yeah. Because, yes, because it was very different than other all you can eat now nowadays, they bring it to you. Yes. I check it. What is missing in this table. So I think mostly, you know the Yelp, Google, they make a review: 'There's one good waiter in the restaurant. He's really nice.' Something like that. That was me. [laughs] I could see that. I could see that. Did you serve LA galbi right from the beginning? Yeah, yeah. So it was already called LA galbi, then? Yeah. Because I'm always curious— Yeah. That is our signature menu. That's right. Yes. Were other places serving LA galbi at the time as well? Do you remember? I don't think so. You don't think so? Yeah. Not all you can eat. Yeah. You know, LA galbi right now, like this, around three piece, going to be $15. So I buy the galbi in the market, cook. My house, that is more expensive than eat it here. Yeah. Because in the house, they bought lettuce. Anything extra, they buy it. Cost not that much. Eating here is cheaper. Yeah. Because I was very curious just about why it became called LA galbi. I don't know why they call—there's many, you know, sauce. Many different sauce, but I don't know which one is good. Yeah. I'm trying to see—I think that's it. I mean, yeah, I just want to talk about the history of it. That's all. That's all I can say, but I think the best way is you try yourself.

organizations.name: Los Angeles Public Library
organizations.description: The Los Angeles Public Library provides free and easy access to information, ideas, books and technology that enrich, educate and empower every individual in our city's diverse communities.

lines.line: # Introduction and Personal Background

> The speaker introduces himself as Mr. King, provides his full name, and shares details about his move from Korea to Los Angeles in 1997 at the age of 25. He recounts his early career at Samsung and the personal motivation behind his immigration, which was to follow his first love, who later became his wife. This chapter sets the stage for the personal and professional journey that follows.

metadata.title: Introduction and Personal Background
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces himself as Mr. King, provides his full name, and shares details about his move from Korea to Los Angeles in 1997 at the age of 25. He recounts his early career at Samsung and the personal motivation behind his immigration, which was to follow his first love, who later became his wife. This chapter sets the stage for the personal and professional journey that follows.

lines.line: # Family Immigration and Early Restaurant History

> The speaker describes his marriage in Korea, his wife's earlier immigration, and the impact of the LA riots on his wife's family. He explains how his father-in-law's restaurant was destroyed during the riots, leading to a disaster loan from FEMA and the opening of a new restaurant in 1992. The speaker, as the eldest son-in-law, took on a significant role in helping the family rebuild and start the business.

metadata.title: Family Immigration and Early Restaurant History
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes his marriage in Korea, his wife's earlier immigration, and the impact of the LA riots on his wife's family. He explains how his father-in-law's restaurant was destroyed during the riots, leading to a disaster loan from FEMA and the opening of a new restaurant in 1992. The speaker, as the eldest son-in-law, took on a significant role in helping the family rebuild and start the business.

lines.line: # Creation and Evolution of the All-You-Can-Eat Korean Barbecue

> The speaker details the innovation of launching the first all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue in 1992. He explains how their buffet-style service allowed customers to pick and cook their own food, differentiating them from other restaurants. The focus was on authentic Korean dishes, and the restaurant aimed to introduce genuine Korean cuisine to a broader audience, resisting the trend toward fusion foods.

metadata.title: Creation and Evolution of the All-You-Can-Eat Korean Barbecue
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details the innovation of launching the first all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue in 1992. He explains how their buffet-style service allowed customers to pick and cook their own food, differentiating them from other restaurants. The focus was on authentic Korean dishes, and the restaurant aimed to introduce genuine Korean cuisine to a broader audience, resisting the trend toward fusion foods.

lines.line: # Business Challenges and Economic Crises

> The speaker recounts the various economic hardships the restaurant faced, including the 1998 IMF crisis in Korea, the 2004 mad cow disease scare, and the 2008 financial crisis. Each event brought significant challenges, such as a drop in travelers and customers avoiding meat, but the business managed to survive through resilience and adaptation.

metadata.title: Business Challenges and Economic Crises
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts the various economic hardships the restaurant faced, including the 1998 IMF crisis in Korea, the 2004 mad cow disease scare, and the 2008 financial crisis. Each event brought significant challenges, such as a drop in travelers and customers avoiding meat, but the business managed to survive through resilience and adaptation.

lines.line: # Naming and Sauce Variations

> The speaker briefly addresses the origin of the name 'LA galbi' and mentions the variety of sauces used in Korean barbecue. He admits uncertainty about which sauce is best, reflecting the diversity of flavors and preferences in Korean cuisine.

metadata.title: Naming and Sauce Variations
metadata.synopsis: The speaker briefly addresses the origin of the name 'LA galbi' and mentions the variety of sauces used in Korean barbecue. He admits uncertainty about which sauce is best, reflecting the diversity of flavors and preferences in Korean cuisine.

lines.line: # Current Struggles and Personal Motivation

> The speaker discusses the current difficulties in the restaurant industry, including rising costs for employees, rent, and materials. He expresses feelings of being overwhelmed and on the brink of collapse, but also shares the satisfaction he gets from making customers happy. Despite the stress, his love for the work and the memory of his wife keep him motivated.

metadata.title: Current Struggles and Personal Motivation
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the current difficulties in the restaurant industry, including rising costs for employees, rent, and materials. He expresses feelings of being overwhelmed and on the brink of collapse, but also shares the satisfaction he gets from making customers happy. Despite the stress, his love for the work and the memory of his wife keep him motivated.

lines.line: # Personal Sacrifices and Family Story

> The speaker reflects on his deep love for his wife, the sacrifices he made to be with her, and the opposition he faced from his family. He describes starting over in America, working menial jobs despite his background, and ultimately considering his marriage the best decision of his life. The passing of his wife in 2019 profoundly affected him, and he made a promise to continue her legacy by running the restaurant.

metadata.title: Personal Sacrifices and Family Story
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on his deep love for his wife, the sacrifices he made to be with her, and the opposition he faced from his family. He describes starting over in America, working menial jobs despite his background, and ultimately considering his marriage the best decision of his life. The passing of his wife in 2019 profoundly affected him, and he made a promise to continue her legacy by running the restaurant.

lines.line: # Taking Over the Restaurant and Learning to Cook

> After his wife's death, the speaker took over the restaurant, facing the challenge of running it without prior kitchen experience. The COVID-19 pandemic forced him to adapt by offering lunchboxes and learning to cook traditional Korean dishes, including kimchi, through self-study and experimentation. Despite receiving government loans and support, he endured significant financial losses but continued operating for the sake of his employees and in memory of his wife.

metadata.title: Taking Over the Restaurant and Learning to Cook
metadata.synopsis: After his wife's death, the speaker took over the restaurant, facing the challenge of running it without prior kitchen experience. The COVID-19 pandemic forced him to adapt by offering lunchboxes and learning to cook traditional Korean dishes, including kimchi, through self-study and experimentation. Despite receiving government loans and support, he endured significant financial losses but continued operating for the sake of his employees and in memory of his wife.

lines.line: # Kimchi and Recipe Development

> The speaker discusses his approach to making kimchi and other Korean dishes, relying on YouTube videos and personal experimentation to develop his own recipes. He takes pride in the positive feedback from customers and has created unique versions of traditional foods like sweet rice drink and cold noodles, often surpassing the quality of previous chefs.

metadata.title: Kimchi and Recipe Development
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses his approach to making kimchi and other Korean dishes, relying on YouTube videos and personal experimentation to develop his own recipes. He takes pride in the positive feedback from customers and has created unique versions of traditional foods like sweet rice drink and cold noodles, often surpassing the quality of previous chefs.

lines.line: # Financial Realities and Cost Management

> The speaker explains the financial pressures of running a restaurant, emphasizing the importance of keeping material costs low. He notes that their high-quality ingredients result in higher costs, which benefits customers but strains profitability. To save money, he personally handles repairs and maintenance, learning from YouTube, but acknowledges that he is reaching his limit in terms of sustainability.

metadata.title: Financial Realities and Cost Management
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the financial pressures of running a restaurant, emphasizing the importance of keeping material costs low. He notes that their high-quality ingredients result in higher costs, which benefits customers but strains profitability. To save money, he personally handles repairs and maintenance, learning from YouTube, but acknowledges that he is reaching his limit in terms of sustainability.

lines.line: # Restaurant Origins and Menu Evolution

> The speaker clarifies the restaurant's history, noting that it originally opened in 1985 as a Korean food buffet, not a barbecue. He describes how the format changed in the early 1990s to include table grills and self-service barbecue, and how his family took over the business from the previous owner. The transition marked the beginning of the all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue concept.

metadata.title: Restaurant Origins and Menu Evolution
metadata.synopsis: The speaker clarifies the restaurant's history, noting that it originally opened in 1985 as a Korean food buffet, not a barbecue. He describes how the format changed in the early 1990s to include table grills and self-service barbecue, and how his family took over the business from the previous owner. The transition marked the beginning of the all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue concept.

lines.line: # Early Customer Base and Menu Additions

> The restaurant quickly became profitable, initially attracting mostly Korean customers. Over time, the menu expanded to include more items such as boneless short ribs, fresh fruit, seafood, and various side dishes. The speaker highlights the evolution of the offerings and the growing diversity of the clientele.

metadata.title: Early Customer Base and Menu Additions
metadata.synopsis: The restaurant quickly became profitable, initially attracting mostly Korean customers. Over time, the menu expanded to include more items such as boneless short ribs, fresh fruit, seafood, and various side dishes. The speaker highlights the evolution of the offerings and the growing diversity of the clientele.

lines.line: # Family Roles and Customer Service

> The speaker explains the division of labor in the restaurant, with his mother-in-law overseeing the taste of the food and his wife managing operations and customer relations. He acknowledges his wife's exceptional ability to connect with customers, a skill he feels he cannot match, despite his own efforts to be friendly and attentive.

metadata.title: Family Roles and Customer Service
metadata.synopsis: The speaker explains the division of labor in the restaurant, with his mother-in-law overseeing the taste of the food and his wife managing operations and customer relations. He acknowledges his wife's exceptional ability to connect with customers, a skill he feels he cannot match, despite his own efforts to be friendly and attentive.

lines.line: # Teaching Korean Barbecue to Customers

> The speaker describes his proactive approach to educating customers about Korean barbecue, offering guidance on how to eat and enjoy the food authentically. He shares anecdotes about helping customers assemble the perfect bite and introducing them to traditional condiments and side dishes, enhancing their dining experience and cultural understanding.

metadata.title: Teaching Korean Barbecue to Customers
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes his proactive approach to educating customers about Korean barbecue, offering guidance on how to eat and enjoy the food authentically. He shares anecdotes about helping customers assemble the perfect bite and introducing them to traditional condiments and side dishes, enhancing their dining experience and cultural understanding.

lines.line: # Customer Experience and Signature Dishes

> The speaker notes that most early customers, being Korean, were already familiar with grilling their own meat. He highlights the restaurant's signature dish, LA galbi, and points out the value of dining at the restaurant compared to preparing the same meal at home. The unique self-service format and quality ingredients set the restaurant apart from competitors.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Invitation

> The conversation concludes with the speaker inviting the interviewer to experience the food firsthand, emphasizing that the best way to understand the restaurant's history and offerings is to try them personally. This closing sentiment encapsulates the speaker's passion for sharing Korean cuisine and culture with others.

children.text: # Introduction and Personal Background

> The speaker introduces himself as Mr. King, provides his full name, and shares details about his move from Korea to Los Angeles in 1997 at the age of 25. He recounts his early career at Samsung and the personal motivation behind his immigration, which was to follow his first love, who later became his wife. This chapter sets the stage for the personal and professional journey that follows.
# Family Immigration and Early Restaurant History

> The speaker describes his marriage in Korea, his wife's earlier immigration, and the impact of the LA riots on his wife's family. He explains how his father-in-law's restaurant was destroyed during the riots, leading to a disaster loan from FEMA and the opening of a new restaurant in 1992. The speaker, as the eldest son-in-law, took on a significant role in helping the family rebuild and start the business.
# Creation and Evolution of the All-You-Can-Eat Korean Barbecue

> The speaker details the innovation of launching the first all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue in 1992. He explains how their buffet-style service allowed customers to pick and cook their own food, differentiating them from other restaurants. The focus was on authentic Korean dishes, and the restaurant aimed to introduce genuine Korean cuisine to a broader audience, resisting the trend toward fusion foods.
# Business Challenges and Economic Crises

> The speaker recounts the various economic hardships the restaurant faced, including the 1998 IMF crisis in Korea, the 2004 mad cow disease scare, and the 2008 financial crisis. Each event brought significant challenges, such as a drop in travelers and customers avoiding meat, but the business managed to survive through resilience and adaptation.
# Naming and Sauce Variations

> The speaker briefly addresses the origin of the name 'LA galbi' and mentions the variety of sauces used in Korean barbecue. He admits uncertainty about which sauce is best, reflecting the diversity of flavors and preferences in Korean cuisine.
# Current Struggles and Personal Motivation

> The speaker discusses the current difficulties in the restaurant industry, including rising costs for employees, rent, and materials. He expresses feelings of being overwhelmed and on the brink of collapse, but also shares the satisfaction he gets from making customers happy. Despite the stress, his love for the work and the memory of his wife keep him motivated.
# Personal Sacrifices and Family Story

> The speaker reflects on his deep love for his wife, the sacrifices he made to be with her, and the opposition he faced from his family. He describes starting over in America, working menial jobs despite his background, and ultimately considering his marriage the best decision of his life. The passing of his wife in 2019 profoundly affected him, and he made a promise to continue her legacy by running the restaurant.
# Taking Over the Restaurant and Learning to Cook

> After his wife's death, the speaker took over the restaurant, facing the challenge of running it without prior kitchen experience. The COVID-19 pandemic forced him to adapt by offering lunchboxes and learning to cook traditional Korean dishes, including kimchi, through self-study and experimentation. Despite receiving government loans and support, he endured significant financial losses but continued operating for the sake of his employees and in memory of his wife.
# Kimchi and Recipe Development

> The speaker discusses his approach to making kimchi and other Korean dishes, relying on YouTube videos and personal experimentation to develop his own recipes. He takes pride in the positive feedback from customers and has created unique versions of traditional foods like sweet rice drink and cold noodles, often surpassing the quality of previous chefs.
# Financial Realities and Cost Management

> The speaker explains the financial pressures of running a restaurant, emphasizing the importance of keeping material costs low. He notes that their high-quality ingredients result in higher costs, which benefits customers but strains profitability. To save money, he personally handles repairs and maintenance, learning from YouTube, but acknowledges that he is reaching his limit in terms of sustainability.
# Restaurant Origins and Menu Evolution

> The speaker clarifies the restaurant's history, noting that it originally opened in 1985 as a Korean food buffet, not a barbecue. He describes how the format changed in the early 1990s to include table grills and self-service barbecue, and how his family took over the business from the previous owner. The transition marked the beginning of the all-you-can-eat Korean barbecue concept.
# Early Customer Base and Menu Additions

> The restaurant quickly became profitable, initially attracting mostly Korean customers. Over time, the menu expanded to include more items such as boneless short ribs, fresh fruit, seafood, and various side dishes. The speaker highlights the evolution of the offerings and the growing diversity of the clientele.
# Family Roles and Customer Service

> The speaker explains the division of labor in the restaurant, with his mother-in-law overseeing the taste of the food and his wife managing operations and customer relations. He acknowledges his wife's exceptional ability to connect with customers, a skill he feels he cannot match, despite his own efforts to be friendly and attentive.
# Teaching Korean Barbecue to Customers

> The speaker describes his proactive approach to educating customers about Korean barbecue, offering guidance on how to eat and enjoy the food authentically. He shares anecdotes about helping customers assemble the perfect bite and introducing them to traditional condiments and side dishes, enhancing their dining experience and cultural understanding.
# Customer Experience and Signature Dishes

> The speaker notes that most early customers, being Korean, were already familiar with grilling their own meat. He highlights the restaurant's signature dish, LA galbi, and points out the value of dining at the restaurant compared to preparing the same meal at home. The unique self-service format and quality ingredients set the restaurant apart from competitors.
# Closing Remarks and Invitation

> The conversation concludes with the speaker inviting the interviewer to experience the food firsthand, emphasizing that the best way to understand the restaurant's history and offerings is to try them personally. This closing sentiment encapsulates the speaker's passion for sharing Korean cuisine and culture with others.

story.title: LAPL Community Histories -  Chang Hun Kang (Choeng Wun Buffet)
story.description: Chang Hun Kang, owner of Choeng Wun Buffet, is interviewed by Tien Nguyen, Los Angeles Public Library Creator in Residence. The interview was conducted on August 7, 2025 at Choeng Wun Buffet, located at 944 N. Western Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90029.
